content:
Note yet a Medium Member? Read this article for free here.
Picture a portfolio review day at a mid-size film program. Reels come in clean. Color is consistent. Sound mix hits -16 LUFS on the nose. Every cut lands on the beat. The faculty panel looks around the room and something feels off. Not wrong, exactly. Just too even. Nobody can point to a single shot and call it fake. But the whole batch reads like it came out of the same machine, because parts of it did.
That’s the room Roberto Serrano walked into at Brown, minus the film gear.
What actually happened, and what didn’t
Serrano teaches Welfare Economics and Social Choice Theory, a proof-heavy math course he’s run for almost two decades. His usual enrollment sits between 8 and 30 students. This spring it jumped to 86. He gave a take-home midterm, partly to ease pressure after a shooting on campus the previous December had left students shaken about sitting in classrooms together.
